comments.post_body:
Why do people complain that Istio is complicated? It's just a handful of resources, 14 to be precise, including Telemetry, WasmPlugin and the old-school Gateway.

This is to certify that I (still) can type Istio config very fast. In a stressful and less than ideal settings of the exam platform.

On a serious note - I wish I'd done this before working on a large-scale enterprise Istio deployment. I do think Istio's architecture is clean and fairly straightforward. But I've also seen first-hand how wild it gets at scale in real world. What gets lost in the "Istio is too complex" debate is that much of that complexity was already there, the scale and the requirements. Additionally, in many cases Istio was adopted at a time when lot of the functionality which is table stakes today was not available back then.
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to a post about the perceived complexity of Istio, and they share their own experience with a large-scale enterprise deployment, arguing that while Istio's architecture is clean, its complexity often stems from the scale and requirements of the project. They believe that much of the complexity was already present before Istio was adopted, and that it has been unfairly blamed for it.

🌟 Community Spotlight 🌟
Shoutout to Keith Mattix II for his awesome contribution to agentgateway! 🚀

Keith introduced a stateful model configuration option for MCP backends that lets multiple gateway replicas handle requests interchangeably — all without needing shared session state.  This improvement boosts scalability, and simplifies architecture.

This kind of contribution not only strengthens agentgateway’s scalability and resilience, but also shows the power of our open-source community coming together to solve real-world challenges.

Thank you, Keith, for your work, collaboration, and commitment to making agentgateway better for everyone! 🚀

#agentgateway #OpenSource #CommunitySpotlight #Kubernetes #CloudNative #Microsoft #PlatformEngineering

comments.comment_body: I agree the EO itself is unconstitutional but how is it unconstitutional to say that federal judges can’t issue sweeping nationwide injunctions?
comments.post_body: Sotomayor's and Jackson's dissents on the conservative majority's abysmal, poorly-reasoned, and flagrantly unconstitutional birthright citizenship ruling released today are excellent.
comments.context_summary: The commenter is praising the dissents of Sotomayor and Jackson in response to the conservative majority's ruling on birthright citizenship, calling the decision abysmal and unconstitutional. They are expressing support for the dissenting opinions released in response to the ruling.

comments.comment_body:
Keith Mattix II if federal judges cannot issue injunctions to prevent federal law from being violated, then what purpose do they serve?

Countless federal judges have issued national injunctions against every administration, including the immediate last one, and up to this point, and the SCOTUS never chose to do anything about it, contest it, or challenge it.

Why pick now, and this issue specifically, to do a 180?

If national injunctions were wrong, they were wrong 10 years ago. 5 years ago. 6 months ago. The SCOTUS has explicitly permitted the practice the entire time. Members of the current majority have upheld the practice in the past.

Should we go back and reverse all the global injunctions federal courts imposed during the Obama era? The Biden era? The previous Trump admin? Even if we should, I think it's pretty clear none of the majority would be interested in doing that.

I don't think it's realistic to pretend that this is a procedural point that's unrelated to the issue that prompted it - if it were, the procedural point would have been resolved decades ago, in one of the hundreds of times it's come up.

comments.comment_body:
🐙Benjamin Leggett "If they were wrong, they were wrong 10 years ago. 5 years ago. 6 months ago. The SCOTUS has explicitly permitted the practice the entire time."

Exactly!! - it was always an abuse of judicial power, and it got really pervasive during the Obama administration as they pushed the envelopes of presidential power. Nationwide injunctions are a dangerous form of relief and it turns federal judges into defacto policymakers. Conservatives forum shop so they can get a favorable judge and then get that one judge to change the enforcement of a law nationwide. It needed to stop. 

I'll disagree on the SCOTUS permitting the practice; in the last few terms since Barrett joined the court, there have been several signals in lower-profile cases that nationwide injunctions were on their way out. Do I wish, as a matter of policy, that the Trump administration would be disallowed from enacting their blantantly unconstitutional ban on birthright citizenship? 100%. comments.comment_body:
🐙Benjamin Leggett I can totally understand why it appears like that. I think from my perspective, the role of SCOTUS is not to handle cases but rather to answer questions. So when a case comes before them (like the migrant rights one you mentioned), they’re not answering “is this good or right or ethical for Trump to do”, they’re answering “is it unconstitutional for the President to do this”

Many legal scholars (not just conservatives, but mostly originalists) are believers in some form of “unitary executive theory”, the idea that all executive power is vested within the President (which is exactly what the constitution says in Article II). So from a first principles perspective, the President has enormous power to change e.g immigration policies as he sees fit. Indeed, the executive branch is the only branch with all of the power vested in a single person.

So in the specific case of these migrant workers, the court is saying “yes Presidents have this power”. I think too often, the American people look to the courts to prevent bad things from happening, but that’s not their function. Their job is to prevent unconstitutional things from happening.

I’m a free agent! ✨

Like many others this week, my team was eliminated and my role at Microsoft was impacted as part of the recent layoffs.

My thoughts and and support goes out to all of the other folks impacted by the layoffs this week. 

While this chapter is closing, I want to express deep gratitude to all the incredible people who made the last 3.5 years at Microsoft such a meaningful experience. It’s the people that made it special.

Despite the short run, I’m proud of the impact I made. I had the incredible opportunity to work directly with a variety of product groups and executives, contributing insights that led to changes and enhancements across AKS, Arc, and other Azure services.

Some other memorable highlights include collaborating on Learn Live sessions with cloud advocates, attending KubeCon, advising startups through the Microsoft for Startups program, being active in BAM, and appearing on Azure Friday a couple of times.

What I’ll take with me: growth, friendships, and unforgettable experiences working alongside some of the brightest minds in tech.

I’m not entirely sure where the next stop will be, but I know it will involve cloud, cloud native technologies, and some AI. Anyone who knows me knows I already have multiple irons in the fire. 

I enjoy leading high-impact teams and strategic initiatives. If you’re hiring for roles in product management, building AI solutions, multi-cloud, cloud native, GitOps, or platform engineering, I’d love to connect and explore how I can help drive impact at your organization. 

I’m open to new opportunities and my DMs are open. 

---------------
Special shoutout to the talented product managers and engineers I had the honor of leading during my time at Microsoft. 📢 

These folks were also impacted by the layoffs and are tech rockstars. I'd work with them again in a heartbeat. Please connect with them and reach out if you have roles:

Michael Papasevastos
Danial Shakeri
Javier Rodriguez
Avinash Patney

Please like and share this post for maximum reach. Thanks in advance!

#OpenToWork #Microsoft #Azure #Cloud #CloudNative #AI #Multicloud #OpenSource #SaaS #Startups #Leadership #BAM

comments.post_body:
🌐 If Kubernetes was designed in 2025, would we have zero-trust networking enabled by default for the data plane 🤔 ?

I posit 𝐍𝐎𝐓. In one word - DevEx and in two words - Developer Experience 🤖. But there is still hope, keep on reading 😃 !

The 𝐨𝐩𝐞𝐧-𝐛𝐲-𝐝𝐞𝐟𝐚𝐮𝐥𝐭 𝐧𝐞𝐭𝐰𝐨𝐫𝐤𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐦𝐨𝐝𝐞𝐥 in Kubernetes was an 𝐢𝐧𝐭𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧𝐚𝐥 𝐝𝐞𝐬𝐢𝐠𝐧 𝐜𝐡𝐨𝐢𝐜𝐞 (rather than an afterthought) to encourage adoption and reduce friction:
1️⃣ 𝐋𝐢𝐟𝐭-𝐧-𝐬𝐡𝐢𝐟𝐭 𝐰𝐨𝐫𝐤𝐥𝐨𝐚𝐝𝐬 (𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐤𝐧𝐨𝐰𝐥𝐞𝐝𝐠𝐞) - In a VM world, networking is typically open by default inside a data center
2️⃣ 𝐄𝐧𝐜𝐨𝐮𝐫𝐚𝐠𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐫𝐚𝐩𝐢𝐝 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐭𝐨𝐭𝐲𝐩𝐢𝐧𝐠 - if security policies were too restrictive by default, it would make deploying multiple apps too complex and would kill the adoption
3️⃣ 𝐒𝐞𝐜𝐮𝐫𝐢𝐭𝐲 𝐢𝐬 𝐧𝐨𝐭 𝐨𝐧𝐞-𝐬𝐢𝐳𝐞-𝐟𝐢𝐭𝐬-𝐚𝐥𝐥 - the risk appetite, certificates and secrets compliance and governance varies across different industries and organizations

Docker, Inc won the development war for containers providing a super intuitive and straightforward CLI and ecosystem.
Adding 𝐝𝐞𝐟𝐚𝐮𝐥𝐭 𝐝𝐞𝐧𝐲 𝐫𝐮𝐥𝐞𝐬 and/or 𝐢𝐝𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐭𝐲-𝐛𝐚𝐬𝐞𝐝 𝐧𝐞𝐭𝐰𝐨𝐫𝐤𝐢𝐧𝐠 to Kubernetes would have been 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐥𝐚𝐬𝐭 𝐧𝐚𝐢𝐥 𝐢𝐧 𝐢𝐭𝐬 𝐜𝐨𝐟𝐟𝐢𝐧 against Docker Swarm or Mesos for winning the production war for container orchestration and deployment.

Remember Pod Security Policies (PSP) and its evolution to Pod Security Standards (PSS)?
I envision something similar for Kubernetes networking as well.

👉 Here's an alternative design idea (2025 Kubernetes):
Instead of fully open-by-default, Kubernetes could adopt Cloud VM-style networking with different networking profiles, consider this for a baseline:
* Default allow for same-namespace communication (like a VM subnet)
* Default deny for cross-namespace communication (like VPC firewall rules)

And:
* An add-on for mTLS (in the spirit of Gateway API et al.) - via kubectl apply -f

🫥 In 2025, even with all the great OSS/paid solutions out there, zero-trust networking still feels super low-level and we need to know about the existence and high-level mechanics of CNIs / Service Meshes, or policy engines / CEL rules that would generate default deny network policies.

🔮 What do you think? Should Kubernetes introduce built-in security profiles for networking, similar to how Pod Security Standards (PSS) replaced PSP?

#kubernetes #networking #k8s #cni #servicemesh #servicemeshes #kubernetesnetworking #signetwork #cloudnative

comments.comment_body:
Part of the difficulty historically is that pod labels were quickly used as an identity construct in addition to metadata storage; even NetworkPolicy allows using source and destination labels as the basis of user authored policy between services.

If Kubernetes started in 2025, I don’t think we make that mistake. Labels can change during the lifetime of the pod, making them an inappropriate vehicle for identity. Instead, I think something like policy between service accounts would be adopted. 

I very much want to see zero-trust networking become much easier in Kubernetes (that’s why I work on Istio ambient lol), but the APIs of the past constrain us a bit w.r.t solutions. Lior Lieberman and I will be presenting on this topic at maintainer summit during KubeCon EU

3️⃣ 𝐒𝐞𝐜𝐮𝐫𝐢𝐭𝐲 𝐢𝐬 𝐧𝐨𝐭 𝐨𝐧𝐞-𝐬𝐢𝐳𝐞-𝐟𝐢𝐭𝐬-𝐚𝐥𝐥 - the risk appetite, certificates and secrets compliance and governance varies across different industries and organizations

Docker, Inc won the development war for containers providing a super intuitive and straightforward CLI and ecosystem.
Adding 𝐝𝐞𝐟𝐚𝐮𝐥𝐭 𝐝𝐞𝐧𝐲 𝐫𝐮𝐥𝐞𝐬 and/or 𝐢𝐝𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐭𝐲-𝐛𝐚𝐬𝐞𝐝 𝐧𝐞𝐭𝐰𝐨𝐫𝐤𝐢𝐧𝐠 to Kubernetes would have been 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐥𝐚𝐬𝐭 𝐧𝐚𝐢𝐥 𝐢𝐧 𝐢𝐭𝐬 𝐜𝐨𝐟𝐟𝐢𝐧 against Docker Swarm or Mesos for winning the production war for container orchestration and deployment.

Remember Pod Security Policies (PSP) and its evolution to Pod Security Standards (PSS)?
I envision something similar for Kubernetes networking as well.

👉 Here's an alternative design idea (2025 Kubernetes):
Instead of fully open-by-default, Kubernetes could adopt Cloud VM-style networking with different networking profiles, consider this for a baseline:
* Default allow for same-namespace communication (like a VM subnet)
* Default deny for cross-namespace communication (like VPC firewall rules)

And:
* An add-on for mTLS (in the spirit of Gateway API et al.) - via kubectl apply -f

🫥 In 2025, even with all the great OSS/paid solutions out there, zero-trust networking still feels super low-level and we need to know about the existence and high-level mechanics of CNIs / Service Meshes, or policy engines / CEL rules that would generate default deny network policies.

🔮 What do you think? Should Kubernetes introduce built-in security profiles for networking, similar to how Pod Security Standards (PSS) replaced PSP?

#kubernetes #networking #k8s #cni #servicemesh #servicemeshes #kubernetesnetworking #signetwork #cloudnative

If Kubernetes started in 2025, I don’t think we make that mistake. Labels can change during the lifetime of the pod, making them an inappropriate vehicle for identity. Instead, I think something like policy between service accounts would be adopted.

comments.comment_body:
Keith Mattix II 

The other major overhang here is that 'identity' is just not a standardized thing in networking at all. This issue goes way back before K8S. 

If there was a well supported IETF RFC to ask "who is my peer" the network policy ecosystem would have scaffolded around it and things would be much better. The transports would have adjusted to fulfill its API contract. It's even OK if the answer in highly heterogenous environments was "DONT_KNOW"

https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc1413. aint it

In this role, I’ll be working with an incredibly talented team responsible for scaling and evolving Morgan Stanley’s global multi-cloud Kubernetes platform, enabling cutting-edge cloud solutions for the firm.

While I’m excited for this next chapter, I want to take a moment to express my sincere gratitude to Mercedes-Benz USA and my wonderful colleagues at Mercedes-Benz AG in Germany. The incredible opportunities I’ve had to grow professionally over the last couple of years have been invaluable.
One of the most impactful moments of my journey was my trip to Germany 🇩🇪, where I had the privilege of working alongside my German colleagues. That experience was transformative—both personally and professionally. It gave me a global perspective, deepened my technical expertise, and fostered lasting relationships that I will always treasure.

I am so thankful to everyone I’ve had the pleasure of working with at Mercedes-Benz. Your mentorship, collaboration, and support have played a huge role in shaping my journey and preparing me for this exciting new chapter.

I’m looking forward to the challenges ahead and to contributing to Morgan Stanley’s Kubernetes strategy! 😎 🤘

comments.post_body:
This week marks my last at solo.io - it's been a great two years, I've learned a *ton*, enjoyed working with some very smart people, accepted a maintainership for a relatively mature and complex open-source project (Istio), gotten to do lots of interesting low-level Linux stuff, developed a very strong appreciation for both Rust/Tokio, and new respect for *anyone* that takes on the challenge of writing spec-complete `http/2` implementations :P

Extreme shoutout to Lin Sun who gave me this (and many other) opportunities and has been an incredible mentor and boss - Lin is a massive asset to the open-source community as a whole, and someone I genuinely respect and trust on a personal level as well.

solo.io is in a remarkably strong position in the market now - the most powerful OSS K8S gateway offering (kgateway) + the fastest, cheapest and most invisible way to secure cluster traffic with mTLS (Istio Ambient)  - and a ton of great people.

It's been great working with you all, I'm off to Edera to help plug the well-known gaps in K8S workload isolation!
